diagrams.title: Access Matrix Model
diagrams.description: A 2D matrix representation of protection where rows represent domains (subjects/users) and columns represent objects (resources/files). Each cell contains the access rights.
elements: Rows: Domain A, Domain B, Domain C (Users/Processes)
Cells: R (Read), W (Write), X (Execute), Empty (no access)
Example: M[Domain A, File1] = 'RW' means Domain A can Read and Write File1
diagrams.purpose: Visualize the conceptual model of protection - shows who (domains) can access what (objects) with what permissions.

diagrams.title: ACL vs Capability Implementation
diagrams.description: Comparison of two ways to implement the Access Matrix - column-wise (ACL) and row-wise (Capability List).
elements: User A: Capability List = [(File1, RW), (File2, R)]
User B: Capability List = [(File1, R), (Printer, P)]
diagrams.purpose: Understand the two primary implementations of protection - which data structure is associated with what (object vs subject).

diagrams.title: UNIX File Permission Structure
diagrams.description: Shows the structure of UNIX file permissions with owner, group, and others categories, each having read, write, execute permissions.
diagrams.purpose: Understand how UNIX represents file permissions visually and the meaning of each position in ls -l output.
